title: Set Diffusion: Interpolating Token Orderings between Autoregression and Diffusion for Fast and Flexible Decoding
abstract: Discrete diffusion models have steadily improved in quality relative to autoregressive (AR) models. However, these models are normally constrained to fixed-length generation and do not support key-value (KV) caching. Block diffusion partially bridges diffusion and AR by unmasking token blocks left-to-right, but it is still limited to generate fixed-size blocks sequentially. Here, we present a new class of language models, set diffusion, comprised of (i) a likelihood parameterization that factorizes over flexible-position, flexible-length token sets and (ii) a set-causal diffusion architecture that supports KV cache updates after every inference step. By factorizing over token sets instead of fixed-size blocks, tokens can be decoded in arbitrarily-ordered sets, including sliding-window sets, enabling faster inference and support for any-order decoding. Set diffusion achieves better speed-quality tradeoffs on mathematical reasoning, summarization, and unconditional generation compared to prior diffusion language models while offering stronger infilling performance than block diffusion. We provide the code, along with the model weights and blog post on the project page: [https://m-arriola.com/setdlms/](https://m-arriola.com/setdlms/)
decision_comment:
This paper proposes Set Diffusion, a diffusion language modeling framework that replaces fixed block-based decoding with a stochastic  order generation process that interpolates between autoregressive and fully random denoising. The method combines an order-informed noise process with a causal diffusion architecture that enables KV caching under non-deterministic token orderings, allowing decoding in sliding-window token sets. Empirically, the approach is intended to improve the speed-quality tradeoff of diffusion LMs while retaining greater flexibility for infilling and any-order generation than prior block-based approaches.

Reviewers generally found the paper’s core conceptual idea compelling. The most positively received aspect is the decision to interpolate between autoregressive and diffusion-style generation through token ordering, rather than through a fixed block structure. Moreover, the theoretical framing of this paper is greatly appreciated.

The most common weakness is around two perspectives. On one hand, the novelty relative to prior work is questioned, and reviewers wanted a more careful comparison and discussion of related approaches, especially those that also model or learn decoding order. A second major concern is the limited experimental scope, especially regarding scale. Several reviewers noted that much of the evaluation is on relatively small models, and even where the paper includes a 1.7B model, the evaluation appears in narrow scope. Reviewers requested broader downstream results at larger scales, as well as clearer evidence that the method remains competitive as model size increases. 

The final reviewer distribution was two weak accepts and two weak rejects, with no strong accept or strong reject. Reviewers broadly agreed that the method is novel and technically sound. The main concern was the scale of the experiments and the still substantial performance gap to strong autoregressive baselines, rather than any major flaw in the method itself.

In the final author comments, the authors provided more comprehensive results on1.7B-scale experiments, which materially strengthened the empirical case. Based on these additional results, the AC’s view is that the paper is solid and justified at least at the 1.7B scale. While evaluation at even larger scales would still be desirable, the current results are sufficient to validate the correctness and promise of the proposed method. Although bigger scales models are not tested at this moment, it is good enough to demonstrate the correctness of the proposed method. It would be encouraged to add results across different scales in the camera-ready if possible.

reviews.summary: The paper introduces Set Diffusion, a language-modeling framework that aims to bridge AR decoding and masked diffusion by interpolating token generation orderings instead of committing to a fixed block structure. The main idea is to bias decoding toward left-to-right order while still allowing partially parallel, flexible generation. The method combines an order-informed noising process with a causal diffusion architecture, so that the model can retain key advantages of diffusion-style decoding while also supporting KV caching and variable-length generation more like AR models.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
- The paper’s main conceptual choice is interesting: interpolating via token ordering rather than fixed block structure.
- The theoretical mapping from the continuous diffusion NELBO to the any-order AR objective via per-token schedules is interesting.

Weakness:
- Although the paper compares MDLM and Block Diffusion, its main novelty is the "interpolation-via-ordering" idea. Therefore, it would be helpful to include a more detailed comparison or discussion with other "order-agnostic" or "any-order" autoregressive models mentioned in the related work.
- See questions.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
- Could the authors please clarify the primary focus of their novelty claim relative to existing work on order-aware or any-order generation?
- In Appendix I.1, it is stated that the 1.7B model was trained with a batch size of 1. Why was such a small batch size used? A batch size of 1 can create extremely noisy gradients. Did this prevent your model from fully converging? Can you provide results using a larger batch size (or gradient accumulation)?
- Your method requires tuning the unmasking width $w$ and scaling exponent $k$ to balance speed and quality. How sensitive is the model's performance to these specific parameters? Does applying this model to a new dataset or different hardware require an expensive grid search to find the optimal $w$ and $k$?
- Is a "-" missing in the Eq. (3)?
- Proposition 3.1 presents a potentially important connection between the diffusion NELBO and an any-order autoregressive objective. However, I found the proof difficult to fully follow, especially in the final step that rewrites the trajectory-wise token loss into the conditioning form of Eq. (3). In particular, Appendix G seems to reduce the ELBO to a sum over single-coordinate change events along the trajectory, but it is not fully clear to me how this implies the final any-order AR conditioning form, rather than a conditioning on the full diffusion state ($z_{t(i)}$). Could the authors please clarify this step more explicitly, ideally by stating the key intermediate lemma(s) and the precise conditioning argument? It would also be very helpful if the authors could provide a brief high-level takeaway of the proof technique itself.
reviews.final_justification: The rebuttal addresses my main concerns about the experiments.

reviews.summary: This paper studies how to leverage and manipulate masking orders in masked diffusion models (MDLMs) to improve both performance and efficiency. Specifically, the authors first introduce a per-token noise injection approach and, based on this mechanism, propose an order-informed noise process to impose a masking-order prior. The token ordering is further utilized to enable causal masking during training and KV caching during inference, leading to improved computational efficiency.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
**Strengths**

+ Incorporating an ordering prior via a windowed noise scheduler is a creative idea, and it can modulate the level of “autoregressivity” through the window size.

+ The authors leverage token ordering to achieve improved training and inference efficiency via causal attention and KV cache, respectively.

**Weaknesses**

- The paper does not present scaling experiments. The largest model evaluated in the paper appears to be 110M parameters, which is relatively small for demonstrating the capacity of modern language models.

- The presentation of the paper is relatively weak compared to the soundness of the method. It would be helpful to include illustrative figures demonstrating the training and inference processes so that readers can better understand the proposed method.

- The token ordering induced by per-token noise appears to mainly support a left-to-right decoding order prior, which may limit generalization to other ordering priors, such as anchor-guided ordering [1].

[1] Rout, Litu, Constantine Caramanis, and Sanjay Shakkottai. "Anchored diffusion language model."
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. It seems that the KV cache implementation does not explicitly account for mask tokens. As described in Algorithm 1, $x^O$ is a subsequence from o to o+S, which should contain mask tokens. Once a mask token is unmasked, the KV of all tokens should be changed due to the bidirectional attention. Do the authors re-sort the sequence according to the decoding order and place all mask tokens at the end, and then apply the attention pattern in Fig. 8 when evaluating $x_\theta(x^O)$? If so, the paper may benefit from a more detailed explanation of this process.

2. Since the order of tokens in the sequence treated with causal attention is determined by the decoding order, how is the "ordering" of tokens in the prompt $x^{1:P}$ determined during inference? This question arises because the prompt does not undergo a decoding process. Do the authors simply apply left-to-right causal masking for the prompt, use bidirectional attention, or randomly permute the tokens in the prompt (w/o permuting position ids)?

3. A rough perspective on the training efficiency issue of MDLMs lies in the relatively low proportion of tokens that contribute to the training loss. In the proposed training strategy, 50% of the tokens in the training sequences are masked and used to compute the loss (i.e., trained tokens), while computations on the remaining clean tokens do not contribute to the optimization objective (i.e., wasted tokens). In conventional MDLM training, the expected proportion of trained tokens is also around 50%. As a reference, 100% of the tokens are trained in AR models. Could the authors further clarify how the proposed method improves training efficiency under this setting?

4. For Prop. 4.1 and its proof, could authors further clarify or highlight the equations that directly demonstrate the tightness of NELBO? In Appendix A.2, the objective is shown to be equivalent to the AR NLL when s->L. How does this result imply the tightness of NELBO?
reviews.limitations: Some of the points raised in the Key Questions and Weaknesses sections may be considered potential limitations of the work.
reviews.final_justification:
My previous concerns have been fully resolved.

While this approach does not yet bridge the performance gap between DLLMs and ARs thoroughly (which is hard for most DLLMs under fair comparison), its technical novelty and performance gains make a compelling case for acceptance.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes Set Diffusion Language Models (SetDLM), a new class of discrete diffusion models designed to bridge the gap between autoregressive (AR) and diffusion-based decoding. The authors argue that interpolating generation orderings is more effective than the fixed block segmentation used in prior works like Block Diffusion. The approach introduces an order-informed noise process that biases decoding toward left-to-right generation while maintaining flexibility for any-order tasks like infilling. Key technical contributions include a causal diffusion architecture that enables full Key-Value (KV) caching and a token-efficient training algorithm that computes gradients for all tokens in a batch simultaneously. Experimental results across mathematical reasoning (GSM8K), summarization (CNN/DailyMail), and language modeling (OWT, LM1B) demonstrate that SetDLM achieves a superior speed-accuracy tradeoff compared to existing discrete diffusion baselines.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
- The Paper introduces a tunable ordering distribution that recovers masked diffusion under specific per-token noise schedules. This provides a more principled and flexible mathematical framework for interpolating between AR and diffusion than rigid block structures. It allows for a smooth tradeoff between sample quality and parallelization by adjusting the decoding window size $w$.
- The proposed causal architecture and the sampling algorithm (Algorithm 1) demonstrate full KV caching capabilities. This addresses a major limitation of standard diffusion models which typically require bidirectional context and fixed-length vectors. As shown in Table 5, SetDLM achieves 141.8 tokens/sec, outperforming MDLM's 121.6 tokens/sec on LM1B.
- Table 6 reports that the "efficient training" method reduces gradient variance by approximately 3x (from $7.50 \times 10^{-2}$ to $2.73 \times 10^{-2}$). Moving beyond objectives that only update masked tokens improves the utilization of training data. This leads to better likelihood estimation and faster convergence compared to the MDLM baseline.
- Figure 1 and Table 1 show that SetDLM (S≤8) achieves 66.41% accuracy on GSM8K, outperforming Block Diffusion (S=4) at 63.53% with higher throughput. It demonstrates that the theoretical benefits of staggered noise schedules translate to practical reasoning gains. The model shows state-of-the-art speed-quality tradeoffs among discrete diffusion models.

Weaknesses:
- The noise schedule depends on both the unmasking interval width $w$ and the scaling exponent $k$ (Section C). The "ideal schedule" is stated to depend on specific user preferences and hardware capabilities, which may limit out-of-the-box usability. Finding the optimal $w$ and $k$ to satisfy the expected inference budget $\overline{C}$ while maintaining lookahead flexibility adds significant tuning overhead. Provide a sensitivity analysis or a grid search visualization of how varying $k$ independently of $w$ affects performance on a validation set.
- Table 1 shows the AR Transformer at 80.21% accuracy on GSM8K, while the best SetDLM is at 66.41%. While the gap is narrowing, the tradeoff between parallelization and accuracy remains a hurdle for diffusion models in high-stakes reasoning tasks. The "Improved Tradeoff" indicated in Figure 1 still places the diffusion models significantly below the AR star on the y-axis. Evaluate if scaling the model size (e.g., to 7B or 14B) reduces this specific accuracy gap compared to an AR model of equivalent size.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. In Figure 1, there is a clear drop in accuracy as tokens/sec increase. Is there a theoretical "limit" to how much accuracy is sacrificed for a given $\overline{C}$, or do you believe further architecture optimizations could push the SetDLM frontier closer to the AR point? 

2. Most experiments use 110M to 1.7B parameter models. Have you observed any "emergent" behaviors or changes in the noise schedule's effectiveness when scaling to larger architectures?

3. Table 6 shows significantly lower gradient variance. Does this efficiency allow for a reduction in the total number of training tokens required to reach a specific perplexity compared to MDLM? 

4. The paper mentions "any-order decoding up to 25 positions ahead". How was this specific lookahead value chosen, and does increasing it further significantly degrade the quality of the generated text?
reviews.final_justification: I have read the authors' rebuttal carefully and appreciate the clarifications regarding computational constraints, noise schedule parameters, and the supplementary training efficiency data. However, after re-evaluating the manuscript in light of these responses, I will maintain my score of Weak Reject. My primary reservations regarding the empirical tradeoffs and the absolute performance gap remain unresolved.

reviews.summary: This work presents SetDLM, an interpolation between AR and MDLM from the perspective of decoding order AR-ness. With an order-informed noise scheduler and its corresponding diffusion training objective, SetDLM could control the left-to-right bias in decoding and obtain a tighter likelihood bound in training. Based on the any-order AR reparameterization, SetDLM could support full KV caching. Experiments show better performance of SetDLM over another important interpolation method of block diffusion.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
## Strengths

- The order-based interpolation between AR and DLM has several advantages over the block diffusion method, e.g. tighter NELBO and full KV caching.
- Experiments of mathematical reasoning, summarization, and unconditional generation, demonstrates the superiority of SetDLM over the block diffusion baseline.
- The writing and presentation of the method is clear.

## Weaknesses

- The novelty of the proposed method is questionable. The two core contributions in this work are non-uniform noise scheduler and causal diffusion architecture, which are not completely novel ideas. For example, the position-aware noise scheduler and training objective in [1] are very similar to the corresponding ones in this work. Besides, there are also multiple causal DLMs that support full KV caching have been proposed [2, 3]. Therefore, this paper needs more comparisons and discussions on those related works, especially [1].
- As many works are focusing on learning the decoding order (or noise scheduler) of DLMs [4, 5, 6], I have concerns about whether the scheduler only based on left-to-right bias could surpass those learning-based approaches.
- Although the model is scaled up to 1.7B parameters, this paper only includes GSM8K evaluations of the 1.7B model, I think more downstream task evaluations are missing, for example, the likelihood-based commonsense reasoning tasks [7] for 1.7B model or zero-shot language modeling perplexities for small models, which will be helpful to more thoroughly demonstrate the model's performance.
- The reason why SetDLM gains better performance than block diffusion seems unclear, more theoretical analysis of the connection and advantage over block diffusion could be beneficial.

[1] Garg P, Kohli B, Sarawagi S. Masked Diffusion Models are Secretly Learned-Order Autoregressive Models[J]. arXiv preprint arXiv:2511.19152, 2025.

[2] Sahoo S S, Yang Z, Akhauri Y, et al. Esoteric Language Models[J]. arXiv preprint arXiv:2506.01928, 2025.

[3] Li J N, Guan J, Wu W, et al. ReFusion: A Diffusion Large Language Model with Parallel Autoregressive Decoding[J]. arXiv preprint arXiv:2512.13586, 2025.

[4] Peng F Z, Bezemek Z, Patel S, et al. Path planning for masked diffusion model sampling[J]. arXiv preprint arXiv:2502.03540, 2025.

[5] Peng F Z, Bezemek Z, Rector-Brooks J, et al. Planner Aware Path Learning in Diffusion Language Models Training[J]. arXiv preprint arXiv:2509.23405, 2025.

[6] Hong C, An S, Kim M S, et al. Improving Discrete Diffusion Unmasking Policies Beyond Explicit Reference Policies[J]. arXiv preprint arXiv:2510.05725, 2025.

[7] Nie S, Zhu F, Du C, et al. Scaling up masked diffusion models on text[J]. arXiv preprint arXiv:2410.18514, 2024.
